topic: A person learns their apartment building is going condo
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mail slot rattled with a sound that felt amplified in the silence of the hallway. Sarah froze, her hand halfway to the box. It wasn't just the usual junk mail; this envelope was thick, official-looking, bearing the embossed seal of a corporate entity she didn't recognize. Her breath hitched, a shallow, quick intake of air that burned in her chest. She snatched the paper, her fingers trembling so violently she almost ripped the seal. The words swam before her eyes: "Notice of Conversion: Building 4B to Luxury Condominium Status." A cold wave washed over her, stealing the warmth from her skin. She dropped the envelope as if it were hot, backing away slowly, her eyes darting towards the elevator, as if expecting it to suddenly descend and swallow her whole. Every floorboard creak sounded like a footstep approaching.

[story 2]

Mark stared at the glossy brochure spread across his kitchen table. The architectural renderings showed sleek glass and minimalist interiors, a stark contrast to the worn, familiar peeling paint of their hallway. He kept running his thumb over the glossy paper, his jaw tight. "This can't be real," he muttered to the empty room, the sound thin and reedy. He walked to the window, peering down at the neighboring building, suddenly seeing it not as a collection of shared living spaces, but as a fortress. His heart hammered against his ribs, a frantic drumbeat demanding escape. He paced the small living room, his movements jerky and uncontrolled, unable to settle on a single spot. The implications of the change—the loss of anonymity, the sudden imposition of rigid rules—made the air feel thick and suffocating.

[story 3]

"They're changing everything," whispered David, his voice barely audible over the distant hum of the ventilation system. He stood by the entrance, clutching his keys so tightly his knuckles were white. The official notification had landed like a physical blow, shattering the mundane reality of his existence. He turned back toward the apartment door, a deep sense of dislocation gripping him. He didn't move to open it immediately; instead, he pressed his forehead against the cool wood, trying to anchor himself against the dizzying sense of impending upheaval. The thought of having to move, of losing the quiet routine he had built over five years, sent a tremor through his limbs. He squeezed his eyes shut, wishing desperately for the noise of the city to drown out the silent, looming threat of transformation.
